diagnosticQuestions.prompt: What is day trading in one line?
choices: Running a strategy on past charts/data to see how it would have performed. Useful to learn mechanics — but the future is not a replay of the past.

Overtrading, revenge trades after losses, and ignoring a plan. Rules and breaks matter.

teachSlides.title: What is day trading in one line?
teachSlides.body:
Opening and closing trades within the same day (often many times) to try to profit from short price moves — very different from long-term investing.

**Example:** Surfing one wave all morning vs. sailing across an ocean — different sport, different injuries.

teachSlides.title: Why do educators say most day traders should expect a steep learning curve?
teachSlides.body:
Because short-term prices are noisy, fees add up, emotions spike, and luck can fool you until it stops.

**Example:** Winning a few carnival games does not mean you own the carnival.

teachSlides.title: What is a stop-loss (simple idea)?
teachSlides.body:
An order to exit a trade near a chosen price to cap how much you lose if the market moves against you. It does not guarantee a fill at exactly that price in fast markets.

**Example:** Seatbelt: it does not prevent every injury, but it limits how bad the crash can get.

teachSlides.title: What is risk/reward ratio in trading talk?
teachSlides.body:
Comparing how much you might lose on a trade to how much you hope to gain. Pros often talk about small, controlled losses and bigger planned wins — but real life is messy.

**Example:** Betting $1 to try to win $2 is different from betting $10 to win $1.

teachSlides.title: What is backtesting?
teachSlides.body:
Running a strategy on past charts/data to see how it would have performed. Useful to learn mechanics — but the future is not a replay of the past.

**Example:** Practicing a play against old game tape: helpful drill, not a promise of next Friday’s game.

teachSlides.title: Why can “more screen time” hurt traders?
teachSlides.body:
Overtrading, revenge trades after losses, and ignoring a plan. Rules and breaks matter.

**Example:** Studying 16 hours straight before a test — tired brains make sloppy mistakes.

teachSlides.title: What is a fair takeaway from long-term investing teachers like the “15 minutes of lessons” style videos?
teachSlides.body:
Principles repeat: patience, understanding the business, managing emotions, and avoiding arrogance after wins.

**Example:** Great coaches repeat basics — layups, breathing, footwork — not because pros are dumb, because discipline beats tricks.

teachSlides.title: If you remember only three rules before risking real money, what should they be?
teachSlides.body:
(1) Never use money you need for bills soon. (2) Understand what you buy. (3) Know that you can lose — so size your bets and sleep at night.

**Example:** Helmet, lights, brakes — before a night bike ride. Boring saves you.

recapSlide.title: ⚡ Level 4 — Advanced / pro topics
recapSlide.body:
Day trading, strategy discipline, risk/reward, and why most short-term traders struggle.

Start with the first card anytime you need a reset.
